The implementation of such an investment strategy allows you to earn an attractive dividend income today. Also, it secures a steady dividend income growth in the years ahead.

The current composition of the portfolio provides investors with broad diversification across sectors, ensuring a reduced risk level.

The Reasons for Adding Ares Capital To The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio

Due to Ares Capital’s ability to combine dividend income with dividend growth, its attractive Valuation along with its financial robustness, and its ability to reduce portfolio volatility (24M Beta Factor of 0.55), I have already added the company to The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio.

Behind Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) (BRK.A) (which presently accounts for 5.87% of the portfolio), BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) (4.34%), Apple Inc. (AAPL) (3.72%), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) (3.50%) and Realty Income Corporation (O) (3.42%), Ares Capital is presently the sixth-largest position of our dividend portfolio. Ares Capital currently represents 3.03% of the overall portfolio. This allocation remains within the 4% limit I have set for the company.

The Reasons for Considering the Addition of Sixt To The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio

Due to Sixt’s attractive Valuation, its combination of dividend income and dividend growth, and its growth outlook, I consider adding the company to our dividend portfolio as well. The company is on my watch list for potential inclusion but is still not part of our dividend portfolio.

Risk Analysis

A risk analysis of a stock is crucial for investors, since a company with a low risk level offers an enhanced probability of delivering successful investment outcomes. On the other hand, companies with a high risk level often tend to present a reduced chance of favorable investment returns. This is mainly because they are subject to more uncontrollable factors, thus reducing the prospects for attractive investment results.

Risk Analysis – Ares Capital

There are different risk factors you should consider before making the decision to invest in Ares Capital.

One of the risk factors is the intense competition the company faces. Among Ares Capital’s competitors are public and private funds, investment banks, and other BDCs, as well as private equity funds. data.title: A Great Quarter Is A Great Time To Downgrade Mercantile Bank Corporation
data.description:
Things have been going quite well for shareholders of Mercantile Bank Corporation (NASDAQ:MBWM). On October 15th, shares of the company jumped by 2.7% after management announced financial results covering the third quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. Although net profits declined year over year, both revenue and profits came in higher than what analysts anticipated. Naturally, as somebody who's bullish on the business, I'm delighted about this development. You see, back in April of this year, I ended up writing my first article about the company. In that piece, I rated the firm a "strong buy."

This rating was based on the continued impressive growth that the company was exhibiting from a revenue and profit perspective. Deposit growth had returned and shares were attractively priced, both on an absolute basis and relative to similar companies. At the end of the day, things turned out quite well. The stock is now up 26.1% since then. That's well above the 12.9% increase experienced by the S&P 500 (SP500) over the same window of time. The picture is still positive. However, between a slight worsening from an asset quality perspective, the prospect of falling interest rates negatively impacting the company, and how the stock is now priced, I believe that it's appropriate to downgrade it to a "buy."

A look at recent results

Compared to what analysts were expecting, Mercantile Bank did quite well for itself. Before the market opened on October 15th, management announced financial results covering the third quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. Revenue came in at just under $58 million. That's roughly $1.1 million above what analysts were expecting. The revenue reported by the company consists of net interest income, after removing provisions for credit losses, plus non-interest income. It would be helpful to look at both of these in turn.

Net interest income for the company came in at $47.2 million. That's an increase compared to the $45.7 million the company reported just one year earlier. Most of this improvement was because of a year-over-year reduction in the provision for credit losses from $3.3 million to $1.1 million. However, the company also benefited from an increase in its interest-earning assets from $4.92 billion to $5.52 billion. The picture would have been better had it not been for the fact that the firm's net interest margin contracted from 3.98% per annum to 3.52% per annum.

Unfortunately, a decline in net interest margin is something that the institution has been experiencing for some time. After peaking in both the final quarter of 2022 and the first quarter of 2021, it began a consistent decline. One of the reasons why I am choosing to downgrade the stock is because I worry that further drops will occur. This is not baseless speculation or merely projecting the past into the future. It's actually based on management's own analysis. In the image below, you can see what would happen to net interest income in different scenarios, ranging from a 3% drop in interest rates to a 3% increase in interest rates.

As you might recall, in September, the Federal Reserve cut rates by 0.50%. And likely, further rate cuts will come about before too long. It's also worth noting that these scenarios provided by management assume a gradual one-year parallel change in interest rates. There's no guarantee that anything about the current rate cut environment will be gradual. A big reason why the company will suffer as interest rates fall is because 74% of all the loans on its books are variable rates. Furthermore, 88% of all loans will reprice in the next 5 years, with 63% repricing no later than 12 months out from the end of the second quarter. This repricing factors in variable rate loans.

Non-interest income for the institution also increased year over year, climbing from $9.2 million to $9.7 million. This increase was driven mostly by two things. First, service charges on accounts grew from $1.4 million to $1.8 million. And second, mortgage banking income for the institution increased from $2.8 million to $3.3 million.

When it comes to the mortgage banking side of things, it's important to note that Mercantile Bank did exhibit some rather attractive growth in mortgage loan originations. In the third quarter of 2024, the company originated nearly $160 million worth of mortgages. At the same time last year, this number was under $110 million. The company has also been selling these mortgages. In fact, according to management, the third quarter saw the company plan to sell 80% of the loans that it originated during that quarter. That compares to 64% of those that originated one year earlier.

Thanks to the improvement in both net interest income and non-interest income, net profits for the company came in at $19.6 million. This translated to $1.22 per share in earnings, which was $0.05 per share greater than what analysts were forecasting. Despite higher revenue, the company suffered from a rise in certain non-interest expense items. Mostly, this took the form of salaries and benefit costs, which grew from $17.3 million to $20.3 million.

In the first chart in this article, you can see results for the first nine months of the 2024 fiscal year compared to the same time last year. Year over year for the whole window, net profits were down. So this is not a terribly surprising development in my view.

On the balance sheet side of things, the picture is looking quite nice. Deposits for the most recent quarter totaled $4.46 billion. That's comfortably above the $4.15 billion reported for the second quarter of the year. And it's significantly greater than the $3.90 billion the institution reported for the third quarter of 2023. This environment has been difficult for banks, with many of them struggling to keep deposits flat, let alone to grow them. However, Mercantile Bank has proven itself to be an exception to this.

While the value of deposits increased, the same can be said of loans. These grew from $4.38 billion in the second quarter to $4.50 billion in the third quarter. The value of securities has been on a nearly constant incline for a year now, rising from $592.3 million in the third quarter of last year to $703.4 million in the most recent quarter. And cash hit the highest it has been in at least five quarters, totaling $328.5 million this time around. Another great thing to see is continued debt reduction. I wouldn't classify debt as terribly high when it comes to Mercantile Bank. But it is always great to see it drop. In the most recent quarter, it fell to $777.4 million. That's the lowest it has been since it was $760.4 million in the third quarter of 2023.

When it comes to pricing the company, annualizing results for the first nine months of this year would translate to net income for 2024 of $79.3 million. This would result in a price-to-earnings multiple of 9.2. This is well above the 6.9 reading I calculated when I wrote my prior article on the firm. In the chart above, you can see how its current price-to-earnings multiple stacks up against five similar banks. Only one of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is.

Naturally, there are other ways to analyze a financial institution. We can use the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book approaches. In the chart below, I calculated these out and compared our candidate to the same five companies I compared it to already. On a price-to-book basis, only one of the five companies was cheaper than our candidate. And on a price-to-tangible book basis, Mercantile Bank ended up being the cheapest of the group.

All of this is great and, normally, this would all lead me to retain the 'strong buy' rating I had on the stock back in April. However, I am choosing to downgrade it in large part because of a change in asset quality. When I last wrote about the company, it boasted a return on assets of 1.54%. That number has now fallen to 1.35%. In fact, three of the five companies that I compared it to have readings that are higher than this, as demonstrated in the first chart below.

The same thing happened with return on equity, as shown in the second chart below. It dropped from 17.07% to 13.73%. In this case, four of the five companies I compared it to have higher readings.

One last thing I would like to touch on involves exposure to a very concerning type of asset. Over the past few years, really since the COVID-19 pandemic began, concerns regarding bank exposure to office assets began arising. High occupancy rates at these properties and the prospect that the nature of work will only continue to change have led to worries that many owners with mortgages on office assets might face financial difficulties. Fortunately, Mercantile Bank is not terribly exposed to this. While 25% of its non-owner occupied commercial real estate falls under the office category, this translates to only about 6% of all of its loans. Furthermore, another 6% is dedicated to retail, which is an area that I have mixed but slightly negative feelings about. In the grand scheme of things, however, these are not terribly large chunks of exposure. It also helps that loans representing 33% of the office assets are dedicated to medical use in the state of Michigan. Medical office properties should be more stable than other types.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I think that Mercantile Bank is doing a fine job. However, the picture has definitely changed. The valuation has gotten higher because of an appreciation in its share price. Asset quality has dropped, and the company faces risks involving declining interest rates. On the other hand, shares are still attractive, and the balance sheet is growing nicely. Add all of this together, and I believe that it makes for a better "buy" candidate than it does a "strong buy" one.

data.title: Investing In The Recent News-Driven Price Drop With Cirrus Logic
data.description:
Cirrus Logic, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:CRUS) price recently dropped based on what now appears to be short-sided news reports. The news was mixed, most of it very positive. The few negative reports, one from ming-Kuo and two from Barclays, drove the extremely negative movement. With investing, misplaced news can drive prices either higher or lower with a vengeance. Savvy investors must gain skills in discerning between what's real and what isn't. Fortunately, the news contained details for both discerning reality plus providing accurate updates of the September, December, and March quarters. We continue our coverage of Cirrus. The last, Cirrus Logic's Revenue Growth Right On Target, discussed the strong evidence for something near a $1.5 content increase in the new iPhone. Extra, Extra, read all about it! Throw your nickel in the hat, grab the paper and let's go.

A View at the News

First, let's review the news. On September 15, 2024, a few days after pre-orders began, ming-Kuo started the rumor mill with his "lower than expected" since the devices became available to pre-order in the U.S. and dozens of other countries on Friday. Kuo said his data is based on a "supply chain survey" His survey concluding a drop of approximately 10% year-over-year volume was derived from delivery times. He also noted that an additional 6 million units of Pros were also produced. And interestingly, he noted that iPhone unit sales are likely shunted early from late software releases dealing with Apple Intelligence.

DigiTimes, a news entity not known for accuracy, published, "Apple's iPhone 16 has seen lower-than-expected initial sales, deepening concerns for South Korean display manufacturers."

Then, on October 1, super bear, Barclays wrote:

"Based on our recent supply chain channel checks, we believe AAPL may just have cut roughly 3M units at a key semiconductor component in iPhones for the Dec-Q, which if confirmed would be the earliest build cut in recent history,"

A chart of Cirrus from TradeStation Securities shows market reactions.

In the same news cycle, several analysts wrote opposite views. But, before we continue, it's essential to better understand who and why customers buy new iPhones. In order of reason, 44% buy because their phones become obsolete, or not supported, 29% buy because their phone died, and finally only 20% purchase for new features. In essence, 20% of the market is feature venerable.

Now, let's summarily review the news in bullet form:

  • In July, Morgan Stanley noted a strange strength in iPhone 15 builds.
  • Another source found used iPhone sales up 28%.
  • Even another source, stated iPhone 16 builds are targeted at 90 million significantly higher than the 15s.
  • Evercore issued results from a massive-sized survey finding that 68% of the respondents plan to upgrade.
    • This is the highest result in recent history.
  • By the end of the 3rd week, JP Morgan issued a report stating that iPhone 16 Pro lead times had stabilized, a pattern seen with iPhone 12/13 launches.
  • On the 30th of September, KeyBanc reported on their survey concluding that "[d]emand for the iPhone 16 Pro and Max remained relatively healthy".
  • And finally, this headline appeared dated October 9th: "Kuo: iPhone 16 sales matching expectations so far, no cutbacks anticipated."

The individual, who had stated the downdraft, admitted unit sales were normal.

The Cirrus Barclays Downgrade

We have one more bit of important news. Barclays, an Apple Inc. (AAPL) super bear, downgraded Cirrus and Skyworks Solutions, Inc. (SWKS) leaving another competitor of Skyworks, Qorvo, Inc. (QRVO) alone. His reasoning in part was that Cirrus has already had its ASP increase for a few years. We single out this downgrade for a couple of reasons, within his notes are several interesting gems about Cirrus' future and noting the more than interesting timing.

Yet, from the downgrade, we found these gems:

  • He expects a 5% iPhone unit increases down from the market estimate of 10% year-over-year increases.
  • "The bank also mentioned that tailwinds from the PC side will not be enough to drive significant growth."
  • "Cirrus Logic should also see an added camera controller in the iPhone 17, but O'Malley said the story for Cirrus Logic has "largely played out until we have a line of sight on content increases in the [iPhone 18]."
  • Cut the price target to $120.

We find it interesting that Tom O'Malley of Barclays made the cut a day after the stock broke above $120 on the good news from KeyBanc. This isn't the first time Barclays' timing just happens to play out fortuitously.

Estimating Cirrus September, December & March Quarters

Before we start estimating, a few pieces of housekeeping are needed. Each $130 million produces $1.00 share in earnings or $15 in stock price. During periods of new camera product introductions, for approximately three years, total ASPs drift upward at $0.05 - $0.30 per chip.

Now let's continue, in the last article, we wrote,

"We actually believe that September will be closer to $600 million with December being closer to $650 million. Earning's estimated for the rest of the year shown in the next table are:

Earnings * June September December March
Revenue $375 $580 $650 $450
Earnings $1.12 $2.45 $3.0 $1.50

"* Based on margin at 51%, tax rate of 23%, share count of 54 million and an average cash expense of $125 million."

"The total earnings estimate equals $8.0 plus. In our view, Apple will include the new parts only in the high-end phones or approximately 40%."

Of most importance, this estimate excludes iPhone unit growth. It is still not clear whether the new chips are all in all the 16s or just the Pros. For estimates during FY-2025, this isn't important. It matters for FY-2026. Our next task assembles projections for estimating coming quarters. We include a table.

In our past articles, we felt that the increase in content was $1.5 and only in the Pro models. After looking at the line-up with the 16s, we now believe that the new codec and amplifier parts are in all models at approximately $1 in gain. We view earnings increases of $1.25 and $1.50 as more realistic. Yet, in total, earnings could still grow from $8 to $11 per share in the time frame Barclays references. At this point, Cirrus has or will have $20 a share in net cash. It is reasonable to estimate over the next few years earnings will grow toward $11. A price target of $120, net cash $100, places Cirrus' P/E at under 10, extremely cheap. During these so-called stable periods, an additional $3.00 in earnings adds $45 to the price placing a likely price of $160+ possible.

More Topics Going Forward

Barclays added, "CY26 should be another year of strong content uplift," the analysts said." Cirrus telegraphed several times two major upcoming opportunities: a higher ASP camera chip and smart power conversion. With respect to the first, an additional fifty cents to a dollar content increase for the four chips included in the total is likely. It is the second that seems most interesting. Power conversion in smartphones could add $2 in content at $0.75 to a $1.00 per conversion. Barclays is an Apple bear and has never been a favorable analyst for Cirrus, but, in the past, they have smelled out important content changes early. O'Malley knows something and it isn't small. In our view, a power conversion product will in time penetrate a lot more phones than Apple. Cirrus product shifts to the paradigm of being smaller, having fewer parts, state-of-the-art efficiency and lower cost. At 1.5 billion units of smartphones being sold, Cirrus could penetrate the 600 million units four or five years ahead. The addition of an improved camera product plus power could add in the range of $2.5 - $3.0 per phone or $700 in revenue, $4 - $6 in earnings.

We also found this comment at the Oppenheimer conference interesting. When asked by Rick Schafer,

". sort of growing into and especially as you start thinking about this lineup of potential SKUs that you can be selling into a PC. So, I didn't know if you could just touch on why, you don't think that it could be bigger than Android for you . . ."

John Forsyth, the CEO, answered, Yeah. Logic, thus, far has played down the PC potential. It is huge and much larger than $40 million per quarter, the value, management continues to throw out.

And one final topic seems in order. In the past several weeks, employment openings plummeted from in the 80s or higher to the high 30s, most of which are summer college hiring positions. At numbers less than 20, 10 to 15 employees per quarter will bleed through natural attrition. We could conclude Logic is heading mean and lean quickly. We believe otherwise. In the last several conferences, management telegraphed quite clearly that M&A is back on the table and that they have their eyes on something to purchase. The company stopped purchasing a lot of stock lately building cash toward $20 per share. We believe a major merger announcement is coming in the next six months or so. It will be cash and earnings accruable out of the gate.

Risk

Cirrus faces risks with China's political behavior and to some extent Apple unit volume issues, especially in China. The stock price faces risk from rogue, inaccurate news, which just occurred. But we included a lot of news stories to scuffle out the truth. At this point, Apple is selling iPhones at a slightly higher rate. The impact will be positive on Cirrus. With all the evidence now showing a lower ASP gain, next year's revenue growth is likely muted but not stunted. Barclays short-sighted downgrade is unfortunately a reality. This won't be their last time either or has it been their first. We view this as a buying opportunity at a price that could once again head north of $160. It is also true that Barclays, true to form, sniffed something important out. We aren't sure what, but it isn't small. The news was different from casual investors might believe. Did you enjoy the extra extra read all about it? We did! Logic is a buy on value again.
